title: Russian Blood and Treasure: The Ballooning Costs of Putin’s War
gist: New CSIS data (Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe) shows Russia has suffered ~1.4 million battlefield casualties and up to 450,000 deaths since Feb 2022, with monthly losses now exceeding recruitment, advances slowing to WWI-Somme-like rates, and Russian territorial control shrinking on net for the first time since August 2024 — even as Ukraine's AI-enabled drone/missile strike campaign degrades Russian energy, industrial, and logistics targets. The authors argue Putin shows no sign of stopping and recommend the West raise the war's cost via more military aid and tighter enforcement of oil-sanctions evasion.

insights.claim: Russia has suffered approximately 1.4 million battlefield casualties and between 400,000 and 450,000 fatalities in Ukraine between February 2022 and June 2026, according to CSIS's own tracking data.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: Establishes the baseline scale of Russian attrition that underpins every other claim about Kremlin capacity to keep fighting.
insights.locator: According to CSIS estimates, Russian forces suffered approximately 1.4 million

insights.claim: Russian battlefield fatalities in the Ukraine war are more than four times all U.S. war fatalities combined since World War II, and more than nine times all Soviet and Russian fatalities combined since World War II.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: Quantifies just how unprecedented Russia's losses are historically, supporting the case that Russian public tolerance for the war is being tested.
insights.locator: Compared to the United States, for example, Russia has suffered roughly four times

insights.claim: In 2026, Russia's monthly casualty rate of 30,000–34,000 has exceeded its monthly recruitment rate of roughly 27,000 new recruits, implying Russia's deployed force is shrinking on net even as fighting continues.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: A hard ceiling on Russian force generation is now visible in the data — watch Russian mobilization/recruitment policy changes as the next lever Moscow must pull.
insights.locator: Russia's monthly casualty rates of over 30,000 per month in 2026

insights.claim: The Russia-to-Ukraine casualty ratio rose from roughly 2:1–3:1 for most of the war to nearly 8:1 in the first half of 2026, driven largely by Ukraine's expanded use of AI-enabled drones in its air-interdiction campaign.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: Shows AI-enabled drone warfare has materially shifted the attrition math in Ukraine's favor, a leading indicator for where the exchange ratio could go with further drone-tech diffusion.
insights.locator: The Russia-Ukraine casualty rate has likely risen to nearly 8:1

insights.claim: Over 90% of Russian battlefield casualties are now estimated to come from drone strikes rather than direct human-to-human combat.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: Drones, not artillery or infantry, are now the dominant lethality mechanism on this battlefield — a structural shift with implications for how future land wars will be fought and equipped.
insights.quote: over 90 percent of Russian casualties are from drone attacks rather than a result of human-to-human engagements
insights.locator: According to some estimates, over 90 percent of Russian casualties

insights.claim: Putin dramatically scaled back Russia's annual military parade in May 2026 due to concerns about Ukrainian long-range strikes, a rare public signal of strain on the Kremlin.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: A visible crack in the Kremlin's confident public posture — usable as a sentiment/legitimacy indicator alongside the harder casualty and territorial data.
insights.locator: President Vladimir Putin dramatically scaled back his annual military parade

insights.claim: Russian and Ukrainian forces advanced at only 50–90 meters per day in their most prominent offensives in the first half of 2026, a pace comparable to the WWI Battle of the Somme and roughly 30–100 times slower than the 3,000–7,000 meters per day of the war's opening maneuver phase in 2022.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: Confirms the war has settled into a defense-dominant, WWI-style attrition dynamic rather than maneuver warfare, arguing against expectations of rapid front-line shifts.
insights.locator: the opening maneuver phases of the war in Ukraine in 2022 ran from roughly 3,000

insights.claim: It took Russian forces nearly two years (late February 2024 to late January 2026) to advance roughly 50 kilometers and finally capture Pokrovsk, a key Ukrainian logistics hub, at an average rate of about 70 meters per day.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: Illustrates the real time-and-casualty cost of Russia's single largest 2026 territorial objective, a benchmark for pricing any further Russian offensive push.
insights.locator: Russian forces took control of the city in late January 2026

insights.claim: Dense minefields, layered fortifications, and drone saturation across a 20–40-kilometer-wide 'kill zone' along the front line explain why even the side holding offensive initiative cannot generate breakthroughs, forcing Russia into small-group infiltration tactics that trade slower gains for still-high losses.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: The defense-dominant character of this war argues against pricing in rapid territorial shifts for either side when weighing a peace-negotiation or ceasefire timeline.
insights.locator: These slow rates reflect the defensive advantages

themes.theme: Territorial stalemate, not steady Russian advance

insights.claim: Russia's territorial control in Ukraine shrank on net in April and May 2026 — a roughly 400 square kilometer net loss and the first monthly net territorial loss for Russia since August 2024 — contradicting a narrative of steady, uninterrupted Russian gains.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: Front-line momentum has genuinely reversed against Russia; any narrative or trade thesis assuming continued Russian territorial gains should be reassessed against this data.
insights.locator: Russia's territorial control in Ukraine shrank in the spring of 2026

insights.claim: Russia currently controls about 118,000 square kilometers of Ukraine (roughly 20% of the country, about the size of Pennsylvania), of which about 75,000 square kilometers has been taken since the February 2022 invasion.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: Sizes the actual post-2022 territorial gain relative to total occupied area, useful context for evaluating any territorial-concession scenario in ceasefire talks.
insights.locator: Russian forces currently control about 118,000 square kilometers of Ukraine

insights.claim: Territorial exchanges in the first half of 2026 were marginal compared to the war's opening year, when Russia seized roughly 115,000 square kilometers in under five weeks in March 2022 and Ukraine retook more than 75,000 square kilometers by November 2022.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: Puts 2026's grinding, marginal territorial shifts in proportion against the war's opening-year maneuver phase, reinforcing that a decisive breakthrough is unlikely absent a major shock.
insights.locator: At the peak of its initial invasion in March 2022, Russia seized

insights.claim: Ukraine has conducted more than 20,000 strike incidents against Russian energy, industrial, logistics, and military targets since the invasion, using drones and missiles as a substitute for traditional airpower in a modern air-interdiction campaign.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: Shows the scale of Ukraine's deep-strike program has moved well beyond isolated incidents into a systematic interdiction campaign comparable to historical airpower doctrine.
insights.locator: It also collects and analyzes data on over 20,000 incidents

insights.claim: Ukraine's new Hornet drone — a roughly $6,000, one-way autonomous attack drone with up to 150km range built via a Ukraine-U.S. company partnership — uses onboard AI (not satellite-dependent, so jam-resistant) to identify targets, distinguish real targets from decoys, and strike during final approach.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: A sub-$10k AI drone credibly destroying far more expensive Russian assets is the clearest cost-asymmetry data point yet for the loitering-munitions/AI-drone investment thesis, and a template Western defense-tech firms will race to replicate.
insights.locator: One recent development is the Ukrainian fielding of the Hornet

insights.claim: SpaceX's decision to limit Russia's access to Starlink in early 2026 reportedly worsened Russian command-and-control problems and further enabled Ukrainian counterattacks, tying a Western commercial satellite company's access decisions directly to battlefield outcomes.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: Illustrates how commercial space/comms infrastructure has become a de facto lever of war — relevant to how investors should weight SpaceX/Starlink's geopolitical option value.
insights.locator: SpaceX's decision to limit Russia's access to Starlink in early 2026

insights.claim: Ukraine's air-defense shortage — insufficient interceptors such as PAC-3 missiles — is a vulnerability Russia is actively exploiting, per a Ukrainian Air Force spokesman's comparison of intercepting simultaneous missile salvos to a goalkeeper facing ten balls at once.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: Interceptor supply (PAC-3 and similar) remains the binding constraint on Ukraine's air defense — a direct read-through to Western air-defense production and delivery schedules as a determinant of the war's trajectory.
insights.quote: A goalkeeper is standing in the goal, and suddenly 10 balls are flying at him at once. He cannot catch all of them. He can catch as many as he has hands and feet, right?
insights.locator: Russia has exploited a notable Ukrainian vulnerability

insights.claim: Ukraine's deep-strike campaign has degraded Russia's energy refining capacity, caused localized gasoline shortages, largely knocked the Russian navy out of the war, and disrupted logistics, but has not been sufficient to cripple Russia's overall war-making capacity.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: Sets realistic limits on what Ukraine's strike campaign alone can achieve — attrition, not knockout — tempering expectations for how fast strikes could force Russian capitulation.
insights.locator: these strikes have caused operational challenges for Russia

insights.claim: Since World War II, interstate wars have ended via ceasefire about 30% of the time, battlefield victory 21% of the time, and formal peace agreement 16% of the time, framing a ceasefire as the statistically most likely outcome for the Ukraine war.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: Sets realistic base-rate expectations for how this war concludes, useful for scenario-weighting any Russia/Ukraine-exposed trade or hedge.
insights.locator: Since World War II, interstate wars have ended in one of several ways

insights.claim: CSIS recommends the U.S. and Europe raise the cost of the war for Moscow via expanded military aid (including more air defense) to Ukraine and tighter enforcement of sanctions on Russia's oil 'shadow fleet,' including secondary sanctions on banks in China and Hong Kong that facilitate evasion.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: A concrete, actionable policy ask — secondary sanctions on China/Hong Kong banks financing Russian oil evasion is the specific lever to watch for escalation.
insights.locator: The United States and Europe should increase pressure on Russia's oil revenues

insights.claim: Despite catastrophic losses and a strained economy, Putin has given no public indication he intends to slow the war, and Russia can keep fighting so long as he is willing to draw on its deep manpower pool and a war economy that remains strained but has not yet buckled.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: The war's continuation is a political choice by one man, not a hard capacity constraint yet — meaning external pressure (sanctions, aid to Ukraine) is the main lever left to shorten the war.
insights.locator: Despite high losses, however, Russia continues to fight

insights.claim: The authors call it a 'bitter irony' that despite Russia's battlefield and economic vulnerabilities, the U.S. and Europe have not yet fully deployed the economic or military pressure available to them.
insights.speaker: Seth G. Jones and Riley McCabe
insights.so_what: Implies meaningful unused escalation headroom exists on the Western side — a reason not to assume the current sanctions/aid regime represents the ceiling of possible pressure on Moscow.
insights.quote: the United States and Europe have failed to fully wield economic or military pressure
insights.locator: The bitter irony of the Ukraine war
